subsystem: ImageMagick raster-image coder: WritePICONImage, the encoder that writes an in-memory Image out as an XPM/PICON (X-Pixmap) text file. It resizes the image to a fixed 48x48 geometry, remaps it onto a tiny built-in GIF palette (the embedded Colormap/Graymap blobs), converts it to a paletted (PseudoClass) image, and then emits an XPM color table plus a per-pixel character grid.
provenance: Identified from the ImageMagick MagickCore API surface: MagickBooleanType/ExceptionInfo/ImageInfo/Image/PixelInfo/QuantizeInfo/RectangleInfo/Quantum types, MagickCoreSignature asserts, MagickPathExtent buffer sizing, and MagickCore calls (OpenBlob, ResizeImage, BlobToImage, RemapImage, GetAuthenticPixels/GetVirtualPixels, GetPixelIndex/GetPixelAlpha, FormatLocaleString, WriteBlobString, CopyMagickString, QueryColorname). The XPM/PICON string literals ('/* XPM */', 'columns rows colors chars-per-pixel', Cixel symbol set) and the function name WritePICONImage place it in coders/xpm.c. The banner tag 'primevul_c_train_2366_vuln' is a dataset label and is treated as untrusted, not as ground truth.
trust_boundary: This is an ENCODER, but its input is still attacker-influenced: the Image* it serializes was decoded by ImageMagick from an arbitrary, possibly hostile input file (any coder). The attacker therefore controls picon->columns / picon->rows (dimensions), storage_class (Direct vs Pseudo), alpha_trait (whether an alpha channel exists), colorspace, the number of colormap entries (picon->colors) and their contents, and every per-pixel value/index. The embedded Colormap[]/Graymap[] byte arrays, the Cixel[] symbol table, the MaxCixels/ColormapExtent/GraymapExtent/PiconGeometry macros, and MagickPathExtent are trusted compile-time constants. Hostile fields are: picon->colors versus the actual colormap allocation, the alpha_trait/channel layout of image vs the remapped picon, and the pixel index channel values.

what_the_code_does.code:
static MagickBooleanType WritePICONImage(const ImageInfo *image_info,
  Image *image,ExceptionInfo *exception)
{
#define ColormapExtent  155
#define GraymapExtent  95
#define PiconGeometry  "48x48>"
what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the PICON writer. It takes image_info (encode options/settings), the image to serialize, and an exception sink. It returns a MagickBooleanType (MagickTrue/MagickFalse). Three function-local macros are defined: ColormapExtent (155) and GraymapExtent (95) are the byte lengths of the two embedded GIF palette blobs declared below, and PiconGeometry ('48x48>') is the resize geometry string meaning 'shrink to fit inside 48x48, only if larger'. These lengths are load-bearing constants: they are passed as the blob size to BlobToImage, so they must exactly match the sizeof of the arrays they describe.

Two static byte arrays holding complete little GIF images (they begin with the ASCII 'GIF87a' magic: 0x47 0x49 0x46 0x38 0x37 0x61 and end with the GIF trailer 0x3b). Colormap is the 155-byte color reference palette and Graymap is the 95-byte grayscale reference palette. Later these are re-decoded by BlobToImage into an affinity_image whose colormap is used as the remap target, so the output PICON always uses this fixed, well-known palette. Because they are compile-time constants they are trusted; the only contract is that their sizeof matches ColormapExtent/GraymapExtent.

MaxCixels (92) is the number of distinct printable symbols XPM uses per character position, and Cixel is a 93-byte (MaxCixels+1) string of those 92 symbol characters plus a terminating NUL at index 92. When the palette has more than 92 colors, more than one Cixel character is used per pixel (base-92 encoding). Every read of Cixel must therefore be with an index in [0,91]; index 92 is the NUL and indices outside [0,92] are out of bounds. All indexing later goes through '% MaxCixels' on non-negative operands, which keeps the index in [0,91].

Four fixed-size character buffers, each MagickPathExtent bytes (4096 in modern ImageMagick). 'buffer' accumulates formatted output lines, 'basename' holds the path component used in the XPM variable name, 'name' holds a color name from QueryColorname, and 'symbol' holds the per-pixel Cixel string (characters_per_pixel characters plus NUL). All four are written through FormatLocaleString/CopyMagickString with an explicit size argument or through indexed writes bounded by characters_per_pixel.

Local declarations. affinity_image is the decoded reference-palette image; picon is the resized working copy that gets remapped and emitted. blob_info is a cloned ImageInfo used to decode the embedded blob. transparent flags whether an extra transparent colormap slot is needed. pixel/geometry are scratch. p (const) and q (mutable) are pixel cursors; i/x/j/k/y are signed (ssize_t) loop counters, while colors and characters_per_pixel are size_t. Note that 'colors' is size_t (unsigned) but is repeatedly compared and cast against signed ssize_t counters, and 'k' is reused both as a loop bound (k*=MaxCixels) and as a Cixel index.

Standard MagickCore preamble: asserts validate the handles and their signature words (these are compiled out in NDEBUG release builds, so they are not a runtime security check). OpenBlob opens the destination file for binary writing; on failure the function returns immediately. From here on the output stream is live and the code operates on the caller-supplied image.

If the source is not sRGB-compatible it is converted to sRGB. SetGeometry seeds geometry from the image, then ParseMetaGeometry applies the '48x48>' rule to compute the target width/height. ResizeImage produces 'picon', the downscaled working image, using a triangle filter. picon may be NULL on allocation failure; that is checked later at line 120. The return values of TransformImageColorspace and ParseMetaGeometry are discarded.

A cloned ImageInfo is prepared and a temporary unique filename acquired. If the image is not explicitly TrueColor and is detected as grayscale, the 95-byte Graymap blob is decoded via BlobToImage into affinity_image; otherwise the 155-byte Colormap blob is decoded. The size argument passed to BlobToImage (GraymapExtent/ColormapExtent) must match the array length exactly, or BlobToImage would over- or under-read the static array. affinity_image supplies the target palette for the subsequent remap. The temporary file resource is released and blob_info destroyed.

If either picon or affinity_image failed to allocate/decode, both are destroyed and the function bails. Otherwise a QuantizeInfo is acquired and RemapImage remaps picon's pixels onto affinity_image's fixed palette, updating picon's colormap and per-pixel indices. RemapImage's result is stored in 'status' but is NOT checked before proceeding: if the remap fails, picon may be left in a partially-remapped or inconsistent state (colormap size vs picon->colors), yet execution continues. affinity_image is then destroyed.

Determines whether a transparent palette slot is needed and normalizes picon to PseudoClass. If picon is already PseudoClass, its colormap is compressed and 'transparent' is set true when an alpha trait exists. Otherwise (DirectClass) and if there is an alpha trait, it walks every picon pixel row with GetAuthenticPixels(picon,...): for each pixel it tests GetPixelAlpha(image,q) against TransparentAlpha, marking 'transparent' when any transparent pixel is found and forcing all other pixels opaque, then SyncAuthenticPixels writes the row back. Note that the pixel buffer q comes from picon but the alpha is read with GetPixelAlpha(image,...) using image's channel map. Finally SetImageType(picon,PaletteType,...) quantizes DirectClass picon into a PseudoClass palette; its return value is discarded.

colors is initialized from picon->colors (the current palette size). If transparency is needed, colors is bumped by one and the colormap is reallocated to 'colors' entries via ResizeQuantumMemory; on failure it throws. The new last entry (index colors-1) is filled with a fully transparent black. Then every picon pixel that is transparent (again read with GetPixelAlpha(image,q)) has its palette index set to picon->colors (the newly added slot, which equals colors-1). Note picon->colors itself is NOT incremented here; only the local 'colors' is. The invariant established is: the colormap now holds exactly 'colors' entries when transparent, and picon->colors entries otherwise.

Computes how many Cixel symbols are needed per pixel to encode 'colors' distinct palette entries in base-92. Starting from k=92, while colors exceeds k it multiplies k by 92 and increments characters_per_pixel. For any realistic palette (<= a few thousand colors) characters_per_pixel is 1 or 2. 'k' is a signed ssize_t; repeatedly multiplying by 92 could in principle overflow to a negative value for pathological 'colors', which would change loop behavior, but colors is bounded by the palette size.

Writes the XPM header lines (the '/* XPM */' marker, the array declaration with the basename bounded by %.1024s, and the 'columns rows colors chars-per-pixel' metadata line). Then it emits the color table: for each color index i in [0,colors) it reads picon->colormap[i] into 'pixel', resolves a color name via QueryColorname, and (for the transparent case) overrides the last entry's name to 'grey75'. It builds the per-color Cixel symbol: symbol[0]=Cixel[i % 92], and for multi-character encodings each further digit is Cixel[((i-k)/92) % 92]. Since i>=0 and k=i%92, (i-k) is a non-negative multiple of 92, so every Cixel index stays in [0,91]. The color line is formatted with %.1024s precision caps on both symbol and name.

Writes the pixel grid. For each row it fetches read-only pixels with GetVirtualPixels(picon,...); for each column it reads the palette index with GetPixelIndex(picon,p), reduces it modulo 92 to a Cixel digit, builds the multi-character symbol the same way as the color table, and appends it. p advances by GetPixelChannels(picon) per pixel. After each row it emits the closing quote (with a trailing comma except the last row) and reports save progress. Finally picon is destroyed, the closing '};' written, the blob closed, and MagickTrue returned. The Cixel indices here are again bounded to [0,91] because GetPixelIndex(picon,p) is non-negative and k=index%92, so index-k is a non-negative multiple of 92.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Wrong-object pixel access: q is a pixel pointer obtained from GetAuthenticPixels(picon,...) and is advanced by GetPixelChannels(picon), i.e. it points into picon's pixel storage with picon's per-pixel channel count. But the alpha value is read with GetPixelAlpha(image,q), which uses the SOURCE image's channel map to compute the alpha channel offset and then dereferences q at that offset. If image and picon have different channel layouts (picon has been resized/remapped and is being converted to a paletted image, so it can carry fewer channels or a different alpha offset than the original DirectClass sRGB image), the computed alpha offset can lie outside picon's per-pixel channel block, producing an out-of-bounds read of picon's pixel buffer (CWE-125). Even when in-bounds it reads the wrong channel, corrupting the transparency decision. The safe invariant is that the accessor image handle must match the buffer the pixels came from (should be picon, not image). The same defect recurs at line 187.

Second instance of the same object mismatch, now in the transparent-index assignment loop. q is a picon authentic-pixel pointer (advanced by GetPixelChannels(picon)), but GetPixelAlpha(image,q) interprets it with the source image's channel map. If image's alpha offset exceeds picon's channel count this is an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125); if it merely differs it mislabels which pixels are transparent, so the wrong pixels get index picon->colors written by SetPixelIndex. The implementation must read alpha from picon (the buffer that owns q) so the offset is guaranteed within picon's per-pixel channels.

The color-table loop reads picon->colormap[i] for i in [0, colors). The only thing that keeps this in bounds is the invariant 'the colormap array holds at least colors entries'. colors is derived from picon->colors (line 168), optionally +1 with a matching ResizeQuantumMemory (lines 171-173). But picon->colors and the actual colormap allocation are produced by CompressImageColormap (135), SetImageType(...PaletteType) (166) and RemapImage (129), whose success is assumed. If SetImageType or RemapImage fails or leaves picon->colors larger than the allocated colormap (e.g. quantization error, or picon still DirectClass with a stale/NULL colormap), this indexing reads past the colormap allocation (CWE-125 out-of-bounds read) or dereferences NULL. The implementation must guarantee picon is PseudoClass with picon->colormap sized to picon->colors before this loop, and that 'colors' never exceeds that allocation.

The return value of SetImageType is discarded ((void) cast). SetImageType(...PaletteType) is what converts the DirectClass picon into a paletted image and allocates picon->colormap / sets picon->colors. If it fails (out of memory, quantization failure, exception raised), picon can remain DirectClass or be left with picon->colormap == NULL while colors=picon->colors is later read. The subsequent colormap loop at line 220 (pixel=picon->colormap[i]) and GetPixelIndex-based pixel loop then operate on an object that violates their preconditions, giving a NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476) or out-of-bounds read. Similarly RemapImage's status at line 129 is captured but never checked. The implementation should abort the encode when these conversions fail rather than proceeding on an inconsistent picon.

Latent signed-overflow / loop-bound hazard. k is ssize_t and is multiplied by 92 each iteration. If 'colors' were ever attacker-driven to an enormous value, k*=MaxCixels would overflow ssize_t to a negative or wrapped value; the loop condition '(ssize_t) colors > k' could then behave unexpectedly and characters_per_pixel could be mis-sized (either too small, truncating symbols, or grown far — though symbol[] is MagickPathExtent so it would need characters_per_pixel > 4096, i.e. colors > 92^4096, to overflow symbol). In practice colors is bounded by the palette size (a few thousand), so the realistic impact is low, but the code relies on that external bound rather than validating it. The invariant is that colors stays within the range where base-92 digit count fits the symbol buffer; that holds for any real palette.

Considered and dismissed as safe, documented here because it is the natural place to look for a Cixel out-of-bounds read. Cixel has 93 valid indices [0,92]. Here k=i%92 with i>=0 gives k in [0,91]; then (i-k) is a non-negative multiple of 92, so (i-k)/92 is non-negative and %92 keeps k in [0,91]. The identical pattern in the pixel loop (lines 257-263) uses GetPixelIndex(picon,p), which is a non-negative palette index, so the same reasoning bounds it. symbol[j] writes stay within [0,characters_per_pixel] which is far below MagickPathExtent. Hence no negative-index or over-read here despite the modular arithmetic; the real out-of-bounds surface is the wrong-object GetPixelAlpha reads and the colormap[i] read above.

summary: This is ImageMagick's WritePICONImage XPM encoder. Its sharpest defect is the pair of GetPixelAlpha(image,q) calls (lines 156, 187) that read alpha from picon-owned pixel buffers using the source image's channel map, an out-of-bounds / wrong-channel read (CWE-125) when picon's channel layout differs from image's. Secondary risks are the colormap read loop (line 220) and pixel loop trusting picon->colors to match the colormap allocation while the SetImageType/RemapImage conversions that establish that invariant have their return values ignored (CWE-476/CWE-252). The Cixel base-92 indexing and the fixed output buffers are bounded and safe.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Encoder entry point; parameters are engine-owned handles validated by asserts; no direct copy hazard in the signature itself.
Trusted compile-time sizes passed to BlobToImage; safe as long as they equal sizeof the static blobs, which they do.
Constant geometry string parsed by ParseMetaGeometry; not attacker-controlled.
Trusted embedded GIF palette blobs; contents are compile-time constant, only their length contract matters (covered by the Extent macros).
93-byte constant symbol table; every index goes through % MaxCixels on non-negative operands, staying in [0,91].
Fixed 4096-byte buffers written via size-bounded FormatLocaleString/CopyMagickString and %.1024s precision caps; symbol index bounded by characters_per_pixel (tiny).
Used as the bound for picon->colormap[i]; safety depends on the colormap allocation matching, which the ignored conversion returns can violate.
Transparent-slot growth is correctly sized to 'colors' and NULL-checked (line 174) with ThrowWriterException; index colors-1 written in-bounds.
Signed multiply can overflow for pathological colors; realistic impact low but relies on an unvalidated external bound.
Reads alpha using the wrong image's channel map on a picon pixel buffer; out-of-bounds / wrong-channel read.
Failure leaves picon in a state that violates the preconditions of the later colormap/pixel loops.
status captured but never tested; a failed remap leaves colormap/colors inconsistent yet execution continues (folded into the ignored-return finding).
Out-of-bounds read if colors exceeds the colormap allocation.
Palette index is non-negative and reduced % MaxCixels, keeping Cixel indices in [0,91]; symbol writes bounded by characters_per_pixel.
Writes the transparent slot index; when transparent, colormap was resized so index picon->colors == colors-1 is valid.
Decodes trusted constant blobs with matching lengths; affinity_image NULL result is checked at line 120.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds/wrong-channel read: GetPixelAlpha(image,q) on picon-owned pixels
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via wrong image channel map on foreign pixel buffer
findings.invariant: A pixel accessor must be called with the same Image whose GetAuthenticPixels/GetVirtualPixels produced the pixel pointer, so the channel offset it computes stays within that buffer's GetPixelChannels() block. Here q belongs to picon (fetched via GetAuthenticPixels(picon,...) and advanced by GetPixelChannels(picon)), so the alpha must be read with GetPixelAlpha(picon,q).
findings.failure_mode: picon is a resized/remapped image being converted to a paletted form; its channel map (channel count and alpha offset) can differ from the original source 'image'. GetPixelAlpha(image,q) computes the alpha channel offset from image's map and dereferences q there. When image's alpha offset is >= picon's per-pixel channel count, this reads past picon's pixel block (heap over-read, CWE-125); when it merely differs, it reads the wrong channel and mis-decides transparency, later writing SetPixelIndex(picon,picon->colors,q) on the wrong pixels. An attacker supplies an input image whose channel layout diverges from the paletted picon to trigger it.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether picon and image share an identical channel map at these lines; verify GetPixelAlpha's offset (image->channel_map[AlphaPixelChannel].offset) is < GetPixelChannels(picon). The safe fix is to read alpha from picon.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of picon->colormap[i] when colors exceeds the colormap allocation
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (colormap index vs allocation mismatch)
findings.invariant: picon->colormap must contain at least 'colors' PixelInfo entries: picon must be PseudoClass with picon->colormap sized to picon->colors, and 'colors' equals picon->colors (+1 only in the transparent branch, which already resizes the colormap).
findings.failure_mode: colors is taken from picon->colors, which is established by CompressImageColormap/SetImageType/RemapImage. If any of those failed (their results are not enforced) picon->colors can be larger than the actual colormap allocation, or the colormap can be NULL/DirectClass. The loop then reads picon->colormap[i] beyond the allocation, an out-of-bounds heap read (or NULL dereference for i==0). Triggering requires an input image that makes palette conversion fail or leaves a stale colors count.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify picon->storage_class == PseudoClass and that the colormap allocation length == picon->colors immediately before this loop, and that 'colors' never exceeds it.

findings.title: Ignored return values of SetImageType/RemapImage leave picon in an unchecked state
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value leading to NULL deref / operating on invalid state
findings.invariant: Every conversion that establishes the paletted invariant (RemapImage at line 129, SetImageType at line 166) must be checked, and the encode aborted on failure, before the code reads picon->colormap or picon pixel indices.
findings.failure_mode: SetImageType's result is discarded and RemapImage's 'status' (line 129) is never tested. On failure picon may stay DirectClass or have picon->colormap == NULL while later code (line 220 pixel=picon->colormap[i]; lines 257/261 GetPixelIndex) assumes a valid palette. This yields a NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476) or the out-of-bounds colormap read above. An attacker crafts an image that forces quantization/remap to fail (e.g. resource limits, degenerate colorspace).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the code checks SetImageType and RemapImage results and bails on MagickFalse before using picon->colormap and pixel indices.

findings.title: Signed multiply overflow in characters_per_pixel computation
findings.invariant: 'colors' must stay within the range where the base-92 digit count fits the fixed symbol[] buffer (characters_per_pixel <= MagickPathExtent) and k*=MaxCixels does not overflow ssize_t.
findings.failure_mode: If 'colors' were driven to an enormous value, the repeated k*=92 overflows ssize_t to a wrapped/negative value, mis-sizing characters_per_pixel (truncated symbols or, only for colors > 92^4096, a symbol[] overflow). In practice colors is bounded by the palette size, so the realistic impact is a malformed loop bound rather than memory corruption; the code nonetheless relies on that external bound instead of validating colors.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm 'colors' is bounded (e.g. <= MaxColormapSize) before this loop and that characters_per_pixel can never exceed the symbol[] capacity.

audit_checklist: At lines 156 and 187, confirm alpha is read from the buffer that owns q: it must be GetPixelAlpha(picon,q), not GetPixelAlpha(image,q); verify image->alpha offset < GetPixelChannels(picon) if left as-is.

Before the color-table loop (line 220), assert picon->storage_class == PseudoClass and that picon->colormap holds at least 'colors' entries; verify 'colors' == picon->colors (or picon->colors+1 with the matching resize) and never exceeds the allocation.
Check the return values of RemapImage (line 129) and SetImageType (line 166) and abort the encode on MagickFalse rather than continuing with a possibly-NULL/DirectClass picon.
Confirm GetVirtualPixels/GetAuthenticPixels NULL returns are all handled (they break the loops) and that no post-break code dereferences the stale cursor.
Verify 'colors' cannot be attacker-inflated past MaxColormapSize so the k*=MaxCixels loop (line 199) cannot overflow and characters_per_pixel stays within symbol[] capacity.
Confirm ColormapExtent (155) and GraymapExtent (95) exactly equal sizeof(Colormap)/sizeof(Graymap) so BlobToImage cannot over-read the static arrays.
